revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:遍照寺 (沖縄市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|prefecture={{ill|Okinawa|qid=Q328615|1=Okinawa (city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|26.33325|N|127.825361|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}
{{Source Citation|date=February 2014|sort_key=Okinawa Henshō-ji}} {{Japanese Temple |Name = Henshō-ji (遍照寺) |image = {{Image needed|cat=Okinawa City}} |image description = |Location = {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, {{ill|Okinawa (city)|en|Okinawa (city)|qid=Q328615|lt=Okinawa City|1=Okinawa (city)}}, Kubota 1-chōme 2−5 |Latitude degrees = 26 |Latitude minutes = 19 |Latitude seconds = 59.7 |N (North)/S (South) = N |Longitude degrees = 127 |Longitude minutes = 49 |Longitude seconds = 31.3 |E (East)/W (West) = E |Map country code = JP |Mountain name = Kongōzan (金剛山) |Temple title = |Main doctrine = {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} |Sect = {{ill|Tō-ji Shingon|ja|東寺真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tō-ji Shingon|1=Tōji Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10343918}} |Temple Rank = |Principal image = |Year founded = during the {{ill|Keitai|ja|景泰|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keitai|1=Jingtai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q840348}} era (1450–1457) |Founder = |Established by = Tsuruo Oshō (鶴翁和尚) |Revival year = {{ill|1672|ja|1672|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1672|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1000|1=1000 (number)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q43016}} ({{ill|Kangxi|ja|康熙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kangxi|1=Kangxi Era|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1185178}} dd) |Revival by = |Official name = Tō-ji Shingon Kongōzan Henshō-ji (東寺真言宗 金剛山遍照寺) |Also known as = |Sacred sites, etc. = |Cultural Properties = |Official website = http://m-kanasa.jp/index.html |Official website name = Religious Corporation Tō-ji Shingon Kongōzan Henshō-ji (宗教法人 東寺真言宗 金剛山遍照寺) |Map = Okinawa Main Island |Map2 = }}
'''Henshō-ji''' (遍照寺) is a temple of the {{ill|Tō-ji Shingon|ja|東寺真言宗|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tō-ji Shingon|1=Tōji Shingon-shū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10343918}} sect located in {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}} {{ill|Okinawa (city)|en|Okinawa (city)|qid=Q328615|lt=Okinawa City|1=Okinawa (city)}}. Its principal image is {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}}. Its mountain name is '''Kongōzan''' (金剛山).
The shrine {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}} emerged from this one.
== History ==
=== The Rise of {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} Shrines and the Establishment of the Eight Ryukyu Shrines === {{Main|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa}}
It is believed that there was no difference in the status of shrines protected by the royal court between the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} and the {{nihongo|Zenke|禅家}} from the 15th to 16th centuries. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Refn|However, the monks who were favored by the royal government were from Zen sects.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=73-79}}{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=547-557}}。|group=Note}}
The opening of Volume 5 of the " {{ill|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|en|Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|qid=Q11573685|lt=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki|1=Ryūkyū Shintō-ki}} " compiled in 1608, lists the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}}'s {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} ( {{ill|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|en|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|qid=Q3109955|lt=Gokoku-ji Temple|1=Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)}} ), {{ill|Oki Shrine|8=en|10=ja|11=沖宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Shrine|1=Oki-no-gū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11552497}} ( {{ill|Rinkai-ji Temple|8=en|10=ja|11=臨海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinkai-ji Temple|1=Rinkai-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q113578587}} ), {{ill|Futemma-gū|8=en|10=ja|11=普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}} ( {{ill|Jinguji Temple|8=en|10=ja|11=神宮寺_(宜野湾市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jinguji Temple|1=Jingū-ji (Ginowan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11589476}} ), and {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|8=en|10=ja|11=安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū‎|qq=}} (Shintoku-ji Temple), as well as the {{nihongo|Zenke|禅家}}'s {{ill|Shikina-gū|8=en|10=ja|11=識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}} (Jinno-ji Temple), {{ill|Ameku-gū|8=en|10=ja|11=天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}} ( {{ill|Shogen-ji Temple|8=en|10=ja|11=聖現寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shogen-ji Temple|1=Seigen-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q117233662}} ), {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|8=en|10=ja|11=末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}} (Manju-ji Temple), and {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Ukishima Shrine|8=ja|9=世持神社|lt=Choju Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines#Ukishima Shrine|qq=}} (Choju-ji Temple), and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|沖宮|lt=Tenmangū Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Choraku-ji Temple). Of these, three of the seven shrines designated as taisha (large shrines) are from the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} and three are from the {{nihongo|Zenke|禅家}}. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2018}}
However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|en|Invasion of Ryukyu|qid=Q1140134|lt=Invasion of Ryukyu|1=Invasion of Ryukyu}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|qid=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|Shō Hō|en|Shō Hō|qid=Q1041365|lt=1625|1=Shō Hō}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|Shō Ken|en|Shō Ken|qid=Q1061074|lt=New|1=Shō Ken}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|qid=Q843010|lt=Shō Shōken|1=Shō Shōken}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}}, and {{ill|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|lt=the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|{{ill|10=ja|11=琉球八社|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|lt=The Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|qid=Q11390939|lt=Asato-Hachimangū‎|1=Hachiman shrine}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered kanpei-sha{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家}} lineage, are excluded from the kanpei-sha.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-sha|ja|官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kanpei-sha|qq=}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=Note}}
=== Later history === Before the separation of Shinto and Buddhism during the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}} and {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}}, it served as the {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} of the adjacent {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}. It was burned down during the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|qid=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}, but was relocated and rebuilt at its current location after the war. (As of mid-October, Reiwa 5, the main hall is under construction and is expected to be completed in two years.)
== External Links == * {{Official website|http://m-kanasa.jp/index.html|Religious Corporation Tō-ji Shingon Kongōzan Henshō-ji}}
=== Related Articles === * {{ill|List of Buddhist temples in Japan|en|List of Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q10311993|lt=List of Japanese Temples|1=List of Buddhist temples in Japan}}
